questions.question: Define and explain the difference between a plain function and a one-to-one function in terms of their domain and range. Provide an example of each.
questions.hint: A one-to-one function is also known as an injective function.

questions.question: Consider a set A = {1, 2, 3} and a set B = {a, b, c}. Define a plain function f from A to B. Then, define a one-to-one function g from A to B. Show that the function f is not one-to-one, and explain why the function g is one-to-one.
questions.hint: A one-to-one function is injective, meaning that no two elements in the domain map to the same element in the range.

questions.question: Suppose we have two sets A = {1, 2, 3} and B = {a, b, c}. If we define a function f from A to B as follows: f(1) = a, f(2) = b, f(3) = c, identify whether the function f is plain or one-to-one. If it is one-to-one, explain why. If it is not plain, explain why it is not plain. If it is neither plain nor one-to-one, explain why it is neither.
questions.hint: A function is considered plain if it assigns a unique output to each input. A function is one-to-one if no two inputs map to the same output.
